sections.title: -아/어 주세요 (Please Do) and -지 마세요 (Please Don't)
sections.explanation: -아/어 주세요 is used to politely ask someone to do something for you. It combines the -아/어 connective ending with 주다 (to give) in the polite imperative: the literal sense is 'please give me the doing of...' For example: 도와주세요 (please help me), 말해 주세요 (please tell me), 읽어 주세요 (please read it for me). The vowel harmony rule applies: bright vowels (ㅏ, ㅗ) take -아 주세요, and all others take -어 주세요. 하다 verbs become 해 주세요. To politely ask someone NOT to do something, use -지 마세요. Attach -지 to the verb stem and add 마세요: 만지지 마세요 (please don't touch), 걱정하지 마세요 (please don't worry), 가지 마세요 (please don't go). -지 마세요 is the negative counterpart of -아/어 주세요.
sections.tip: -아/어 주세요 literally means 'please give me the favor of doing X.' -지 마세요 is the polite way to say 'please don't.' Both are essential for everyday polite requests in Korean.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Please help me' using 돕다 (to help)?
exercises.explanation: 돕다 is an irregular verb: 돕 + 아 → 도와. So 'please help me' is 도와 주세요. The ㅂ changes to 와 before 아.

exercises.prompt: How do you politely say 'Please don't run' using 뛰다 (to run)?
exercises.explanation: -지 마세요 attaches to the verb stem to make a polite negative request: 뛰 + 지 마세요 = 뛰지 마세요 (please don't run).

exercises.prompt: 이 편지를 읽어 ___. (Please read this letter for me.)
exercises.explanation: 읽어 주세요 is the polite request form: read (읽다) + -어 주세요.

sections.title: -아/어도 되다 (May I) and -(으)면 안 되다 (Must Not)
sections.explanation: -아/어도 되다 is used to ask for or grant permission — 'may I,' 'is it okay to,' or 'you may.' 되다 means 'to be okay/allowed.' The pattern is: verb stem + -아/어도 되다. Examples: 가도 돼요? (May I go?), 먹어도 돼요 (You may eat), 앉아도 돼요? (May I sit down?). The response can be: 네, -아/어도 돼요 (yes, you may) or 아니요, -(으)면 안 돼요 (no, you must not). -(으)면 안 되다 expresses prohibition or that something is not allowed. It literally means 'if you do X, it won't do.' Consonant-ending stems take -으면 안 되다; vowel-ending stems take -면 안 되다. Examples: 여기서 담배를 피우면 안 돼요 (You must not smoke here), 늦으면 안 돼요 (You must not be late).
sections.tip: -아/어도 되다 asks 'is it okay if I...?' while -(으)면 안 되다 says 'you must not / it's not okay to.' They are natural pairs for permission and prohibition.

examples.en: You must not make phone calls during class.

exercises.prompt: How do you ask 'May I open the window?' using 열다 (to open)?
exercises.explanation: -아/어도 돼요? asks for permission: 열다 → 열어도 돼요? (May I open?). 창문을 열어도 돼요? = May I open the window?

exercises.prompt: What does 늦으면 안 돼요 mean?
exercises.explanation: -(으)면 안 되다 means 'must not / is not allowed.' 늦으면 안 돼요 = You must not be late.
-아/어도 돼요? is the permission question. 먹어도 돼요? = May I eat?

sections.title: -(으)ㄹ까요? (Shall We? / Do You Think?)
sections.explanation: -(으)ㄹ까요? has two main uses: making suggestions ('shall we...?') and asking for opinions or speculation ('do you think...?'). Formation: verb stem ending in vowel + -ㄹ까요?, verb stem ending in consonant + -을까요?. For suggestions: 같이 갈까요? (Shall we go together?), 뭐 먹을까요? (What shall we eat?), 커피를 마실까요? (Shall we drink coffee?). For asking opinions: 내일 비가 올까요? (Do you think it will rain tomorrow?), 이 색이 어울릴까요? (Do you think this color suits me?). When used with the first person singular (제가/내가), it offers to do something: 제가 도와드릴까요? (Shall I help you?). This is a very common and versatile ending in conversational Korean.
sections.tip: -(으)ㄹ까요? is softer than a direct question — it invites the other person's input. Use it for polite suggestions, offers to help, and when wondering about something together.

examples.en: Do you think the weather will be nice tomorrow?

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Shall we go together?' using 가다 (to go)?
exercises.explanation: 가다 stem is 가 (vowel ending), so add -ㄹ까요?: 갈까요? 같이 갈까요? = Shall we go together?

exercises.prompt: What does 제가 문을 닫을까요? mean?
options: You must not close the door.
exercises.explanation: 제가 + -(으)ㄹ까요? means 'shall I...?' — offering to do something. 제가 문을 닫을까요? = Shall I close the door?
마시다 stem is 마시 (vowel ending) → 마실까요? 뭐 마실까요? = What shall we drink?
